title: service-accounts — subscribed events
description: service-accounts listeners are focused on: (a) cache invalidation whenever an SA changes state, (b) cascading kid rotation when the auth module rotates a signing key, (c) tenant lifecycle propagation. The module is a substrate; it does not consume unrelated events.

listeners.purpose: Synchronous cache eviction on rotation so a request in flight can't reuse a stale cached row for JWT verification.
Same as above — disable propagates within one cache TTL. Also invalidates the `svc:signer_kid:{kid}` cache entry so batch-revocation views refresh.
Same eviction pattern on soft-delete + hard-delete phases.
Synchronous append to `svc:last_used:{service_account_id}` cache buffer. The UpdateLastUsedTimestampJob flushes to Postgres. Fire-and-forget on the request hot path.

When the auth module rotates the key material for a specific kid (e.g. periodic key hygiene), enumerate every SA whose signer_kid = <rotated_kid> and either (a) leave them pointing at the new material (transparent — kid stayed the same), OR (b) if the auth module DEPRECATED the old kid and provisioned a new one, coordinate an SA-side kid update. Emits `stackra.service_account.kid_rotation.propagated` metric.
listeners.notes: The auth module's kid-rotation event fires with a `mode` payload field: 'in_place' (kid stayed, material rotated) or 'renamed' (kid replaced). This listener only takes action on 'renamed' mode.

listeners.purpose: When the auth module disables a kid (typically incident-response), enumerate every SA whose signer_kid = <disabled_kid>, mark them for operator review (metadata flag `signer_kid_burned = true`), and dispatch SuspiciousServiceAccountActivityNotification to platform-security naming the affected SAs. SAs are NOT auto-disabled — the kid burn revokes their JWT signatures, so they can't issue new tokens, but the SA rows remain in the table for triage.

When a Tenant is being erased (compliance-driven full-tenant delete), enumerate every SA scoped to the tenant (`tenant_id = <erased_tenant>`) and soft-delete them alongside. Platform-plane SAs (`tenant_id IS NULL`) stay put.

When an Application is disabled (rare — typically a product sunset event), soft-disable every SA bound to `application_id = <disabled_application>`. Existing JWTs remain valid until natural expiry; the disable prevents new token-exchange.

notes: Every listener above is idempotent. A retry after partial success doesn't double-fire downstream effects.

Cache-invalidation listeners run synchronously (queue: null) so the state is guaranteed consistent by the time the emitting request returns to the caller. The tradeoff is a per-write cache-write cost — acceptable because writes on the SA table are rare (a few per day, not per second).
